name: Los Angeles: The Glamour of Hollywood Boulevard

stops.name: Hollywood Walk of Fame
stops.shortDescription: Comprising over 2,700 five-pointed terrazzo and brass stars embedded in the sidewalks. It serves as an evolving public monument to the achievements of the entertainment industry.

stops.name: TCL Chinese Theatre
stops.shortDescription: Opened in 1927, this exotic movie palace has hosted countless glamorous film premieres. Its concrete forecourt bears the handprints, footprints, and signatures of Hollywood's biggest legends.

stops.name: Dolby Theatre
stops.shortDescription: The permanent home of the annual Academy Awards ceremonies since 2002. Its grand staircase is lined with glass pillars displaying the names of every Best Picture winner in Oscar history.

stops.name: El Capitan Theatre
stops.shortDescription: A fully restored, opulent 1926 movie palace now operated by The Walt Disney Company. It features elaborate pre-show entertainment and a massive Wurlitzer pipe organ with over 2,500 pipes.

stops.name: Hollywood Roosevelt Hotel
stops.shortDescription: A historic Spanish-style hotel that hosted the very first Academy Awards in 1929. Marilyn Monroe lived here for two years, and her ghost is rumored to still haunt a full-length mirror in the lobby.

stops.name: Madame Tussauds Hollywood
stops.shortDescription: The renowned wax museum where visitors can interact closely with uncannily realistic figures of celebrities. It famously lacks velvet ropes, encouraging guests to touch and pose directly with the displays.

stops.name: The Hollywood Museum
stops.shortDescription: Housed in the historic Max Factor building, it contains the most extensive collection of Hollywood memorabilia in the world, including iconic costumes, props, and personal artifacts.

stops.name: Egyptian Theatre
stops.shortDescription: Opened in 1922, it was the site of the first-ever Hollywood film premiere. Recently restored, its stunning Egyptian Revival architecture kick-started the trend of lavish movie palaces.

stops.name: Musso & Frank Grill
stops.shortDescription: Hollywood's oldest restaurant, founded in 1919. It retains its classic red leather booths and was famously frequented by stars like Charlie Chaplin, Marilyn Monroe, and numerous literary giants.

stops.name: Hollywood & Vine
stops.shortDescription: The legendary intersection that was once the epicenter of the radio and film industries. Today, it remains a famous cultural touchstone representing the golden age of old Hollywood.

stops.name: Capitol Records Building
stops.shortDescription: An iconic mid-century modern circular building designed to look like a stack of records on a turntable. It houses the legendary recording studios used by Frank Sinatra and The Beatles.

stops.name: Pantages Theatre
stops.shortDescription: A magnificent Art Deco theater that formerly hosted the Academy Awards in the 1950s. Today, it is the premier destination in Los Angeles for major Broadway touring productions.

stops.name: Funko Hollywood
stops.shortDescription: A massive 40,000 square foot experiential retail store celebrating pop culture, featuring incredible larger-than-life photo ops with beloved movie, TV, and comic characters.

stops.name: Amoeba Music
stops.shortDescription: The world's largest independent record store, offering a massive, vibrant space filled with millions of new and used CDs, vinyl records, and fascinating pop culture items.
